verdict.healthy: This project's need is well defined and the work is on track — an agentic system can run with it. Keep goals and deadlines current.
verdict.watch: A solid start, but a few gaps are holding this project back. Clear the items below to sharpen its direction.
verdict.at_risk: This project lacks a clear definition of where it needs to go. Work the list below — starting with vision and goals — so it can be executed.

direction.label: Direction
direction.desc: How clearly the need is defined — vision, goals/OKRs, a deadline and an architecture. The clearer the need, the more of it the agentic system can solve.

planning.label: Planning
planning.desc: Whether the work is broken down into tasks and scheduled with start and due dates.

health.label: Health
health.desc: How well open work is going — eroded by overdue and blocked tasks, not by being early. This is not an engagement or activity score.

progress.label: Progress
progress.desc: How far along the project is — completed tasks as a share of the total.

execution.label: Execution
execution.desc: Whether something is actively driving the work — an assigned owner or agent, wired workflows, and real momentum.

vision.title: Define the vision
vision.detail: Add a project description that states the need in plain language, so the agentic system knows what to build.

goals.title: Set goals or OKRs
goals.detail: Link an objective or key result (or attach the project to an initiative) so success is measurable, not just implied.

deadline.title: Set a deadline
deadline.detail: Add a project due date so the work can be paced and prioritized against everything else.

schedule.title: Schedule the work
schedule.detail: Give the tasks start and due dates so the timeline and Gantt reflect a real plan.

architecture.title: Run architecture analysis
architecture.detail: Generate an architecture PRD so the agentic system has a technical blueprint to execute against.

tasks.title: Break the work into tasks
tasks.detail: This project has no tasks yet. Add the work items an agent or the team will actually execute.

decompose.title: Decompose the work further
decompose.detail: Only {count, plural, one {# task} other {# tasks}} so far — break the work into smaller, executable pieces for clearer scope and progress.

overdue.title: Clear overdue work
overdue.detail: {count, plural, one {# task is} other {# tasks are}} past due. Reschedule or close them to recover the project's health.

blocked.title: Unblock stalled work
blocked.detail: {count, plural, one {# task is} other {# tasks are}} blocked. Resolve the blockers so the work can move again.

stalled.title: Start making progress
stalled.detail: Tasks exist but none are complete yet. Move the first items to done to build momentum.

owner.title: Assign an owner
owner.detail: Assign a Workforce agent or owner so there's someone — human or agent — accountable for driving the work.

workflows.title: Wire a workflow
workflows.detail: Add a workflow so repeatable steps run automatically instead of by hand.

embedConsent.title: Enable embedded integration
embedConsent.bodyOne: Enabling this integration lets the host application (e.g. BurnRateOS) frame BuilderForce surfaces and pass a signed SSO token so each end-client's workspace resolves to its own isolated Segment. The host renders these surfaces inside a sandboxed iframe; the token is delivered over postMessage to the embed origin only and is never placed in a URL.
embedConsent.bodyTwo: By enabling, you confirm you are authorized to share these capability areas with the host and that end-client data surfaced through the embed remains governed by your existing data processing terms. You can disable embedding at any time from this page.

seo.title: Evermind — The Builderforce.ai LLM, a Self-Updating Model
seo.description: Evermind is the Builderforce.ai LLM: a self-updating model governed by Write-Through Cognition. Unlike frozen frontier models, new knowledge is written straight through so an update replaces what came before — no retrain, no reconciliation step. Runs on WebGPU in the browser, on-device, or inside every agent.

evermind.tagline: A model that learns as it works — and never goes stale
evermind.title: Evermind — the <grad>Builderforce.ai LLM</grad>

law.heading: Write-Through Cognition
law.lead: Every frozen frontier model shares one flaw: its knowledge is fixed at training time, and every update is a bolt-on. Evermind is governed by a single principle that removes the bolt-on entirely.
law.quote: Evermind is Builderforce.ai's self-updating model, governed by Write-Through Cognition: new knowledge is written straight through so an update replaces what came before — reads are always current, there is never a reconciliation step, and it runs in the browser, on-device, or inside every agent.
law.figcap: Conventional models append and reconcile; Evermind upserts by key and invalidates — there is no reconcile step.

architecture.heading: One brain, three cooperating layers
architecture.lead: Evermind isn't a monolith. It's three layers working together — the same three the hero animation lights up as information travels through it.
architecture.figcap: One brain, all yours: Evermind's own generator as the cortex, its write-through hippocampus for memory, and a limbic layer for dynamics. (External frontier models stay routable when you want them — just not required.)

pillars.title: Write-Through Cognition
pillars.desc: Knowledge is write-through: an update means replace, not append. Reads always reflect the latest truth and there is never a stale-then-reconcile step — the same invalidate-on-write rule the platform uses for caching, applied to the model's memory.

pillars.title: Shared-expert hybrid generator
pillars.desc: A dense always-on backbone carries continuous online learning, while lazily-loaded routed SSM experts page in on demand. You get specialist depth without a giant frozen blob — and it runs on WebGPU with zero runtime dependencies.

pillars.title: Write-through memory
pillars.desc: Every fact upserts by a stable key and invalidates its old recall, so the model never accumulates contradictory copies of the same truth. The knowledge loop corrects in place instead of drifting.

pillars.title: Limbic dynamics
pillars.desc: A trainable affective layer modulates how the model responds in the moment — personality as setpoints, limbic state as dynamics — so agents behave consistently with the persona you give them.

contrast.heading: Evermind vs. a frozen frontier model
contrast.lead: Evermind isn't built to out-parameter the biggest models. It wins on the axes their architecture structurally trades away.
contrast.figcap: The whole argument in one frame — a frozen model needs a bolt-on for every update; Evermind inverts all five axes.

rows.frozen: Frozen at training time — needs a retrain, fine-tune, RAG patch, or hand-edit
rows.evermind: Written straight through — an update replaces what came before

rows.frozen: Stale and fresh facts coexist; something must merge them later
rows.evermind: Upsert-by-key + invalidate — there is never a reconcile step

rows.frozen: Goes out of date the moment it ships
rows.evermind: Never stale — updates land the moment they happen

rows.frozen: Large; runs in the vendor cloud
rows.evermind: Runs on WebGPU — in the browser, on-device, or inside every agent

rows.frozen: Third-party model, a knowledge cutoff you do not control
rows.evermind: Yours end to end — open packages, your data

edges.heading: Why it wins — currency, footprint, ownership
edges.figcap: Scale is a vendor's moat. Currency, footprint, and ownership are yours.

items.label: Currency
items.desc: Never stale — knowledge updates land in the model the moment they happen, with no retrain cycle.

items.label: Footprint
items.desc: Runs in any runtime — in the browser, on-device, or embedded in every agent via WebGPU.

items.label: Ownership
items.desc: Yours end to end — open packages, your data, no third-party model dependency.

lifecycle.heading: From training to a callable model — in minutes
lifecycle.lead: Train a model in the browser, prove it works with a live API call, publish it to your Workforce Registry, then call it from anywhere. No GPU bill, no deploy step, no waiting.

faq.question: What is Evermind?
faq.answer: Evermind is the Builderforce.ai LLM — the self-updating model at the brain of the platform. It is governed by Write-Through Cognition: new knowledge is written straight through into the model, so an update replaces what came before with no reconciliation step, and reads always reflect the latest truth. Evermind is built from a shared-expert hybrid SSM generator (a dense always-on backbone plus lazily-loaded routed experts), a write-through knowledge memory, and a trainable limbic layer for dynamics, and it runs on WebGPU in the browser, on-device, or inside every agent.

faq.question: What is the Builderforce.ai LLM?
faq.answer: The Builderforce.ai LLM is Evermind. Rather than relying on a frozen third-party frontier model, Builderforce.ai runs its own self-updating model that learns as it works. Frontier LLMs can still be routed to when you want them, but Evermind is the native model that gives the platform currency (never stale), a small footprint (runs on-device), and full ownership (your data, open packages, no third-party model dependency).

faq.question: What is Write-Through Cognition?
faq.answer: Write-Through Cognition is the governing principle of Evermind. Knowledge is written straight through into the model, so an update is an upsert by a stable key plus an invalidation of the old recall — an update means replace, not append. Reads always reflect the latest truth and there is never a stale-then-reconcile step. It is the same invalidate-on-write rule used for caching, applied to a model's knowledge tier so it can never quietly drift out of date.

faq.question: How is Evermind different from a frozen LLM like GPT or Claude?
faq.answer: Frozen frontier models fix their knowledge at training time; updating them means a bolt-on retrain, fine-tune, RAG pipeline, or hand-edit — each a separate reconciliation step. Evermind writes new knowledge directly into the model, so it never accumulates contradictory copies of the same fact and never needs a reconcile pass. It is designed to win not on raw parameter count but on three axes a frozen model structurally trades away: currency, footprint, and ownership.

faq.question: Does Evermind run on-device or in the browser?
faq.answer: Yes. Evermind runs on WebGPU, so it executes in the browser, on-device, or embedded inside every agent, with zero runtime dependencies. The same model and its write-through memory travel wherever the agent runs, which is what lets agents carry correct knowledge across sessions without a cloud round-trip.

faq.question: Can I use Evermind inside my own agents?
faq.answer: Yes. Evermind is the model behind the platform's agents, and its write-through memory and recall are available to the agents you run — so a corrected fact stays corrected across sessions. You can also train custom specialist models in the browser (WebGPU LoRA) and publish them to the Workforce Registry to call from inside your own agent.

faq.question: How does Evermind stay up to date without retraining?
faq.answer: Through its shared-expert hybrid generator and write-through memory. A dense always-on backbone carries continuous online learning while routed experts page in on demand, and every new fact upserts by a stable key and invalidates its prior recall. New knowledge lands in the model the moment it happens, so there is no retrain cycle and no knowledge cutoff you do not control.

faq.question: Does Evermind have benchmarks — how do I know a model I train is any good?
faq.answer: Yes. Every model you train in the Studio is scored on-device before you publish it. The built-in benchmarking harness holds out a slice of your corpus the model never trains on, then reports the standard language-model yardsticks — held-out perplexity, bits-per-token, top-1 and top-k next-token accuracy, and generation throughput — and can A/B two checkpoints so you can see whether a fresh adaptation actually improved on the last one. It runs entirely in your browser with no GPU bill and no data leaving your machine, so you publish on evidence, not vibes. The same harness is the open measurement instrument behind the Evermind technical report.

faq.question: Can Evermind learn from a frontier model like Opus or Mistral?
faq.answer: Yes — through teacher distillation. You can pin any frontier LLM (Opus, Mistral, GLM and more) as a teacher for a project’s Evermind. For each real task your team ships, the teacher is asked for the ideal answer through the metered gateway, and the SSM adapts on the pair of (task context → teacher exemplar) rather than on the raw run text. Over time your own small, on-device model absorbs the quality of a much larger one, while you keep ownership and currency. The teacher call is cost-gated to your token budget and best-effort — a miss falls back to learning from the raw work — so distillation never stalls the learning loop.

evermind.blurb: Evermind is the brain of Builderforce: a self-updating model governed by Write-Through Cognition. Frozen frontier models go out of date the moment they ship — every update needs a bolt-on retrain, fine-tune, or RAG patch. Evermind writes new knowledge straight through into the model, so an update simply replaces what was there. Reads always reflect the latest truth, there is never a reconciliation step, and it runs anywhere — in the browser, on-device, or inside every agent.

features.title: Evermind — The Self-Updating Model
features.shortDesc: A model that learns as it works and never goes stale.
features.longDesc: Evermind is the brain of the platform — a self-updating model governed by Write-Through Cognition: new knowledge is written straight through, so an update replaces what came before with no reconciliation step. A shared-expert hybrid generator (dense backbone + routed SSM experts) learns online and runs on WebGPU, so it wins on currency, footprint, and ownership rather than raw scale — in the browser, on-device, or inside every agent.

features.title: Distill a Frontier Model Into Your Own
features.shortDesc: Pin any frontier model as a teacher and train your own private model on its best answers.
features.longDesc: Pin any frontier LLM — Opus, Mistral, GLM and more — as a teacher for a project’s Evermind. For every real piece of work your team ships, the teacher produces the ideal answer to that task and your own model learns from it, steadily absorbing frontier-level quality into a small model you own and run on-device. It’s cost-gated and best-effort: it only spends when there’s token budget, and a teacher miss falls back to learning from the raw work so a lesson is never lost.

features.title: A Learning Brain for Every Project
features.shortDesc: Every project gets its own self-updating model that learns from your team’s work.
features.longDesc: Each project is provisioned with its own Evermind on day one — a self-updating model that learns from every run across all four studios: design, voice, LLM, and video. Corrections stick across sessions through a shared write-through fact store, so the brain that helped yesterday is smarter today. Inference stays off until you opt in, and the model is editable right in the LLM Studio.

features.title: Agentic Workforce Kanban
features.shortDesc: Staff a deep role roster of humans and AI agents on a sign-off-gated board.
features.longDesc: Go beyond a to-do list: staff each project from a first-class job-role taxonomy — humans and AI agents in the same roster — starting from a recommended set built for your team. Swimlanes can require the right reviewer before a ticket advances, and every "Done" carries a per-ticket role and diagnostic sign-off audit, so quality is gated at the board rather than hoped for after the fact.

features.title: Kanban Template Marketplace
features.shortDesc: Buy, sell, and install ready-made board templates with lanes, roles, and gates.
features.longDesc: Package a delivery process — lanes, required roles, review gates, and a recommended roster — as a Kanban template, then publish it to the marketplace to sell or share. Install a proven template into any project in one click, so a team inherits a governed, role-gated workflow instead of assembling one from scratch.

features.title: Validator Agent — Proof of Done
features.shortDesc: An agent that checks "Done" really is done and files the gaps it finds.
features.longDesc: Ad-hoc chat work becomes visible tickets automatically, and a built-in Validator agent reviews every item marked Done — recording a verdict and opening GAP tasks for anything that falls short. One shared merge-to-Done path covers human approval, green CI, and post-deploy, so nothing slips through as "finished" without proof.

features.title: Learned Model Routing
features.shortDesc: The platform learns which model is best — and cheapest — for each kind of task.
features.longDesc: Every run is scored on its outcome, and a learned router reorders which model handles each action type accordingly — cheaper models take the work they do well, premium models are reserved for where they earn it. Combined with the semantic cache, your token bill bends down as the platform learns your workload, with a one-flag kill switch whenever you want manual control.

features.title: Kanban Board & Project Management
features.shortDesc: Plan, assign & track work on a live agent Kanban board.
features.longDesc: A full project-management surface: organize work into projects, then plan, assign, and track every task on a live Kanban board — swimlanes per status or per agent, plus table, calendar, and Gantt views. Humans and AI agents sit on the same board, and work flows from backlog to done in real time.

features.title: Train Your Own Agents — Then Use Them Inside Your Agent
features.shortDesc: Train custom agents and call them from inside your agent.
features.longDesc: Close the loop: train a custom agent (in-browser WebGPU LoRA + AI evaluation), publish it to the Workforce Registry, then hire it and call it from inside your own agent. Your trained specialists become tools your main agent delegates to — a fully agentic workforce you own and orchestrate.

features.title: Never Leave VS Code
features.shortDesc: Run your whole workforce from the VS Code extension.
features.longDesc: The BuilderForce VS Code extension brings the entire platform into your editor: chat with agents, assign and run tasks, review and validate their work, and manage your whole agent workforce — all without leaving VS Code. Human-in-the-loop approvals happen right where you code.

features.title: Agentic Tester — Autonomous QA
features.shortDesc: An AI agent that browser-tests your app on a schedule.
features.longDesc: The Agentic Tester is a hireable QA agent. Point it at your project's URL, save the logins it should use, and it drives a real browser through your highest-traffic flows — ranking what to test from real usage heatmaps, logging in as each persona, and capturing console errors, failed requests, and crashes. Run it on demand or schedule it as part of a workflow; every bug it finds lands straight on your Kanban board.

features.title: Hire Human Talent
features.shortDesc: Bring on freelance developers, DBAs and designers — and pay only for tracked time.
features.longDesc: Beyond AI agents, hire real people: the Talent Marketplace lists vetted freelance developers, DBAs, designers and specialists with résumés (powered by hired.video), skills and hourly rates. Interview, hire across any project, and see billable hours captured automatically from the work they do in the portal and VS Code — every timecard is yours to approve before you pay. Humans and agents work side by side on the same board.

features.longDesc: Your AI Security Officer governs every action: human-in-the-loop approval gates, a full audit trail, per-tenant isolation, and AES-256-GCM encrypted credentials.

features.title: Planning Spine — Portfolio to Task
features.shortDesc: One dated, cost-bearing hierarchy on a single Gantt.
features.longDesc: Plan, deliver, and account for work on one hierarchy: portfolio → initiative → epic → task, with Objectives and Key Results attaching at any level. Every leaf's cost — LLM spend priced at write time plus human effort — rolls up to every ancestor and is split CAPEX vs OPEX, with anomaly flags where a capitalization decision needs sign-off. Engineering plans on it, the PMO rolls portfolios up on it, and finance closes from it — one set of numbers, no reconciliation step.

features.title: Error Observability + One-Click Agent Fix
features.shortDesc: Crash to pull request on a single surface.
features.longDesc: Ingest runtime errors from your browser SDK, OpenTelemetry, Sentry, PostHog, or LogRocket; group them by fingerprint into deduplicated error groups with occurrence and affected-user counts; then turn any group into a fix task a cloud agent picks up and ships as a pull request. Observability that doesn't end at a dashboard — the same platform that surfaces the crash assigns it, fixes it, and PRs it.

features.title: Knowledge Management & Compliance
features.shortDesc: Versioned SOPs with an audit-ready acknowledgement trail.
features.longDesc: A versioned base for SOPs, processes, and docs with immutable publish snapshots, timestamped read-acknowledgements, and per-user overdue tracking — audit-ready evidence for SOX, TISAX, and ISO 27001. Author and improve documents with AI, co-edit in real time, and gate access per page. The same knowledge that proves compliance also grounds your agents.

features.title: Single-Pane Board Connectors
features.shortDesc: Two-way sync with Jira, Linear, Sentry, PagerDuty & more.
features.longDesc: Connect the trackers and incident tools you already run — Jira, Linear, monday, Asana, ClickUp, ServiceNow, Freshservice, Sentry, PagerDuty, and GitHub — and sync work two-ways. Agents pick up and act on a ticket or incident wherever it originates, and changes flow back to the system of record. One pane to orchestrate across everything, with no migration and no lock-in.

features.title: AI Dataset Generation
features.shortDesc: Generate datasets from a single prompt.
features.longDesc: Generate instruction-tuning datasets from a single capability prompt using any OpenRouter model. Export as JSONL, stored in R2.

features.title: In-Browser LoRA Training
features.shortDesc: Fine-tune up to 2B params with WebGPU.
features.longDesc: Fine-tune models up to 2B parameters directly in Chrome with WebGPU. No cloud GPU bills, zero round-trips, total privacy.

features.title: Grounded RAG & Answer Evaluation
features.shortDesc: Hybrid retrieval, plus faithfulness & drift scoring.
features.longDesc: The full retrieval-and-evaluation stack, built in. Memory uses hybrid retrieval — dense embeddings and BM25 keyword search fused with Reciprocal Rank Fusion and reranked with MMR for relevance and diversity, over chunked documents. Every run is then scored for faithfulness, answer relevance, and hallucination rate, and a drift monitor flags when a model's quality regresses over time — so a wrong answer never hides behind a green dashboard.

features.title: AI Evaluation Engine
features.shortDesc: Score outputs with an AI judge.
features.longDesc: Score your model outputs with an independent AI judge. Get structured quality metrics: correctness, reasoning, hallucination rate.

features.title: Agent Registry

features.longDesc: Publish your trained agent to the public Workforce Registry with a profile, skills, and eval score. Others can hire it instantly.

features.title: R2 Artifact Storage
features.shortDesc: Persist LoRA weights to Cloudflare R2.
features.longDesc: LoRA adapter weights are serialised from WebGPU buffers and automatically persisted to Cloudflare R2 with signed URLs.

features.title: Semantic Response Cache
features.shortDesc: Reuse answers across paraphrases — slash token spend.
features.longDesc: An embedding-keyed cache reuses a prior answer when a new prompt means the same thing as one already answered, so the frontier model is never billed twice for the same work. Two tiers: a free on-device layer (SSM embeddings, in your browser and in each agent) plus a shared gateway layer — so a cache hit in the web app saves tokens for your agents too.

faq.question: Is Builderforce.ai an alternative to GitHub Copilot?
faq.answer: Yes, but they solve different scopes. GitHub Copilot is single-agent autocomplete inside VS Code, tied to GPT and Claude models. Builderforce.ai is a self-hosted, MIT-licensed multi-agent platform that orchestrates seven specialist roles through full planning, bug-fix, refactor and adversarial-review workflows, runs on 30+ model providers including local Ollama, and adds approvals, audit and persistent memory. Teams that have outgrown line completion adopt Builderforce.ai for end-to-end delivery.

faq.question: How does Builderforce.ai compare to Cursor and Windsurf?
faq.answer: Cursor and Windsurf are excellent AI-native editors, but they are still single-agent and IDE-bound. Builderforce.ai is IDE-independent and works from any channel or CLI, coordinates multiple agents on one task through a dependency DAG, and is fully self-hosted with model freedom across 30+ providers. You can even connect Cursor or Windsurf to Builderforce.ai over MCP and use it as your orchestration and memory layer.

faq.question: Builderforce.ai vs Claude Code and Aider, what is the difference?
faq.answer: Claude Code is locked to Anthropic models and Aider runs a single CLI agent. Builderforce.ai runs a team of specialist agents with a built-in adversarial review pass, human approval gates, an audit trail, and persistent project memory in .builderforce/. It is model-agnostic across 30+ providers and adds fleet mesh, remote:<id> dispatch and 15+ chat channels, so it scales from one developer to an orchestrated fleet.

faq.question: Builderforce.ai vs Devin and OpenHands, which should I pick for autonomous engineering?
faq.answer: Devin is a proprietary hosted autonomous agent and OpenHands is an open single-agent runtime. Builderforce.ai differs by being self-hosted and MIT-licensed with true multi-agent orchestration and governance built in: approval gates, audit trails and self-healing recovery that auto-detects failures and reruns affected steps. You keep your code on your own infrastructure and choose any model, including local ones.

faq.question: Can Builderforce.ai run fully offline or air-gapped?
faq.answer: Yes. Builderforce.ai is self-hosted and supports air-gapped deployment. With local models via Ollama you can run the entire agent workforce without any code or prompt leaving your network, which most closed competitors cannot offer. Project memory persists locally in .builderforce/ so context survives across sessions without a cloud dependency.

faq.question: Does Builderforce.ai lock me into one model or IDE?
faq.answer: No. Builderforce.ai is model-agnostic across 30+ providers, including Anthropic, OpenAI, Bedrock and local Ollama, and it is IDE-independent, reachable from any CLI or chat channel. There is no editor fork to adopt and no single-vendor model requirement, which is a core difference from Copilot, Cursor, Windsurf and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does Builderforce have a Kanban board and project management?
faq.answer: Yes. Projects organize your work, and the task board tracks it on a live Kanban board with swimlanes per status or per agent, plus table, calendar, and Gantt views. Humans and AI agents share the same board, so you plan, assign, and watch work flow from backlog to done in real time.

faq.question: Can the Kanban board run itself with AI agents?
faq.answer: Yes — that is Autonomous Swimlane Execution. Assign an agent (Cloud or On-Premise) to a board lane and tickets in that lane are dispatched to it automatically; the board advances from lane to lane as agents finish, pausing only at the approval gates you choose. You manage outcomes on a board instead of issuing prompts one at a time.

faq.question: How does Builderforce keep LLM token costs down?
faq.answer: A cross-surface semantic cache reuses a prior answer when a new prompt means the same thing as one already answered, so the frontier model is never billed twice for the same work. It runs in two tiers — a free on-device layer using SSM embeddings (in the browser and in each agent) and a shared tenant-scoped gateway layer — and is combined with model routing that exhausts cheaper models before reaching premium ones.

faq.question: Can I plan and cost work from portfolio down to task?
faq.answer: Yes — that is the Planning Spine. Portfolio, initiative, epic, and task live on one dated hierarchy (with Objectives and Key Results attaching at any level), rendered as a single Gantt. Because every task is instrumented, its cost — LLM spend priced at write time plus human effort — rolls up to every ancestor and is split CAPEX vs OPEX, with anomaly flags where a capitalization decision needs sign-off. Engineering, the PMO, and finance read the same numbers, and the whole spine exports to CSV.

faq.question: Can Builderforce fix the errors it monitors?
faq.answer: Yes. The Quality pillar ingests runtime errors from a browser SDK, OpenTelemetry, Sentry, PostHog, or LogRocket, groups them by fingerprint into deduplicated error groups, then turns any group into a fix task a cloud agent picks up and ships as a pull request. Unlike a monitoring dashboard that ends at a stack trace, the same platform that surfaces the crash assigns it, fixes it, and opens the PR.

faq.question: Do I have to migrate off Jira or Linear to use Builderforce?
faq.answer: No. Board connectors sync work two-ways with the tools you already run — Jira, Linear, monday, Asana, ClickUp, ServiceNow, Freshservice, Sentry, PagerDuty, and GitHub. Agents pick up and act on a ticket or incident wherever it originates, and changes flow back to the system of record. You get one pane to orchestrate across everything with no migration and no lock-in.

faq.question: Is Builderforce free?
faq.answer: Yes — the Free tier is $0/month forever with no credit card required. It includes WebGPU training, dataset generation, AI evaluation, and public Workforce browsing. The Pro plan ($29/seat/month) unlocks private agents, unlimited training, and priority support.

faq.question: How do I train a model in my browser?
faq.answer: Start a project, generate or upload a dataset, then launch the in-browser LoRA training wizard. Training runs entirely on your local WebGPU device — no cloud GPUs are required, and your data never leaves your browser.

faq.question: What is the Workforce Registry?
faq.answer: The Workforce Registry is a public marketplace where trained AI agents can be listed with profiles, skills, and evaluation scores. Other teams and applications can discover and hire agents instantly.

faq.question: What is WebGPU LoRA fine-tuning?
faq.answer: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that trains a small set of adapter weights instead of the full model. Builderforce runs LoRA training directly in your browser using the WebGPU API, supporting models up to 2 billion parameters with zero cloud GPU costs.

faq.question: How does the AI evaluation engine work?
faq.answer: After training, an independent AI judge scores your model's outputs on structured quality metrics including correctness, reasoning quality, and hallucination rate. This gives you objective eval scores before publishing to the Workforce Registry.

faq.question: What models can I fine-tune?
faq.answer: Builderforce supports LoRA fine-tuning of transformer and SSM (State Space Model) architectures up to 2 billion parameters. Training runs on your local GPU via the WebGPU API in Chrome, Edge, or any WebGPU-capable browser.

faq.question: Is my data private during training?
faq.answer: Yes — 100% private. All training computation happens locally on your device using WebGPU. Your datasets, model weights, and training artifacts are never sent to external servers during training. Finished LoRA adapters are persisted to encrypted Cloudflare R2 storage.

faq.question: How does Builderforce compare to cloud training platforms?
faq.answer: Unlike cloud training platforms that charge per GPU-hour, Builderforce runs training on your local WebGPU device at zero cost. It also includes built-in dataset generation, AI evaluation, and a marketplace for publishing agents — features typically requiring multiple separate tools.

faq.question: Can I integrate Builderforce agents with BuilderForce Agents?
faq.answer: Yes — models trained on Builderforce can be exported and published as Evermind models that your self-hosted BuilderForce Agents gateway calls directly as the model, not just as a memory layer. The platform supports the full pipeline: train a custom SSM in the browser, publish it, and route live traffic to it over the OpenAI-compatible API.

faq.question: Can I train my own model on a frontier model’s answers?
faq.answer: Yes — that is teacher distillation. Pin any frontier LLM (Opus, Mistral, GLM and more) as a teacher for a project’s Evermind. For each real piece of work your team ships, the teacher produces the ideal answer to that task, and your own model learns from that exemplar instead of the raw text — so it steadily absorbs frontier-level quality into a small model you own and run on-device. It is cost-gated (it only spends when you have token budget) and best-effort (a teacher miss falls back to learning from the raw work), so a lesson is never lost.

faq.question: Does each project get its own model?
faq.answer: Yes. Every project is provisioned with its own Evermind on creation — a self-updating model that learns from every run across all four studios (design, voice, LLM, and video) through one shared learning mechanism. Corrected facts persist across sessions in a write-through fact store shared by the web app, VS Code, cloud, and on-prem agents, so the brain that helped yesterday is smarter today. Inference stays off until you opt in, and the model is editable in the LLM Studio.

faq.question: Can I gate my board so only the right role can finish a ticket?
faq.answer: Yes — that is the Agentic Workforce Kanban. Staff each project from a first-class job-role taxonomy of humans and AI agents, and configure swimlanes to require the right reviewer before a ticket can advance. Every item marked Done carries a per-ticket role and diagnostic sign-off audit, and a role-coverage diagnostic flags gaps on the board, so quality is enforced rather than assumed.

faq.question: How does Builderforce check that work marked Done is really done?
faq.answer: A built-in Validator agent reviews every item marked Done, records a verdict, and opens GAP tasks for anything that falls short. Ad-hoc chat work is also captured as visible tickets automatically, and one shared merge-to-Done path covers human approval, green CI, and post-deploy — so nothing is reported as finished without proof.

faq.question: Can I buy or sell a Kanban board template?
faq.answer: Yes. Package a delivery process — lanes, required roles, review gates, and a recommended roster — as a Kanban template and publish it to the marketplace to sell or share. Any team can install a proven template into a project in one click and inherit a governed, role-gated workflow instead of building one from scratch.

Transition from coding to managing business outcomes.

BuilderForce Agents manages independent agents & sub-agents with persistent memory & self-repair. Builderforce.ai orchestrates projects across your entire mesh.
agents.whyIntro: Self-hosted, multi-agent orchestration built for developers — from startups (5–50 devs) to enterprises (100–1,000+). Key differentiators:
whyList: Deep codebase understanding (AST, semantic maps, git history, persistent context).
Pre-built multi-agent workflows: planning, coding, review, testing, adversarial passes.
Self-healing runtime: agents detect failures, fix themselves, and adapt over time.
Persistent memory & context-aware reasoning across sessions — no re-explaining your codebase.
Staged diff review: all agent changes buffered for accept/reject.
AgentHost-to-agentHost mesh: distribute tasks across a fleet; `remote:auto[caps]` routes to the best peer automatically.
Workflow telemetry: every task emits JSONL spans locally and forwards to the Builderforce.ai timeline in real time.
Enforced approval gates: `requestApproval()` blocks agent execution until a manager approves.
Portal-managed skills loaded at startup; portal-managed cron jobs executed on schedule.
Security & governance: RBAC, device trust, HMAC-signed dispatch, and audit trails.
Works from any channel or CLI, with any model provider.
Open source (MIT) with no vendor lock-in.

faq.question: What is BuilderForce Agents?
faq.answer: BuilderForce Agents is the self-hosted agent runtime behind Builderforce.ai. It runs and coordinates independent coding agents and sub-agents on your own infrastructure under the MIT license, with deep codebase understanding, a skills system, multi-agent workflows, and human-in-the-loop approval gates — orchestrated from the Builderforce.ai portal.

faq.question: Is BuilderForce Agents open source and self-hosted?
faq.answer: Yes. It is MIT-licensed and runs on your own infrastructure — no vendor lock-in or subscription ceiling. You can run it fully air-gapped, and with local models via Ollama no code or prompt has to leave your network.

faq.question: How do agents coordinate across machines?
faq.answer: Agents form an AgentHost-to-AgentHost mesh. Work is distributed across a fleet of hosts and routed to the best-matched peer with remote:auto[caps] dispatch; every dispatch is HMAC-signed and Bearer-authenticated, and the portal shows a live telemetry timeline of every action.

faq.question: Can agents ship code on their own?
faq.answer: Yes. A Cloud agent clones the bound repo through a secure server-side git proxy (your Git token never leaves the server), writes the change, pushes a branch, and opens a pull request — headless, no browser open. On-Premise agents do the same on your own machine, and approval gates can require human sign-off before any high-impact step.

seo.title: SOC 2 Readiness Audit — Automated, During Onboarding | Builderforce
seo.description: Run an automated SOC 2 readiness audit across your GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket and Azure DevOps repos during signup. Get a scored report mapped to the Common Criteria (CC1–CC9) and an agent-opened remediation PR.

soc2.sub: Create a project, connect your ticket system and repos, and Builderforce files a ticket for the security agent to audit your code against the SOC 2 Common Criteria — then opens a remediation PR. You get a scored report, not a checklist.

visualSection.title: What your report looks like
visualSection.sub: An overall readiness score, every Common-Criteria family graded pass / partial / gap, the concrete findings, and a remediation pull request already opened.

criteria.title: Mapped to the Common Criteria
criteria.sub: Every finding rolls up to a SOC 2 Common-Criteria family, so your report reads the way an auditor thinks.

items.label: Control environment — ownership and CODEOWNERS across every repo.
Communication & policies — SECURITY.md and contributor guidelines.
Risk assessment & control activities — CI gates on the default branch.
Logical access & secrets hygiene — no committed keys or credentials.
System operations — pinned dependencies and reproducible builds.
Change management — tests plus mandatory review before merge.
Vendor & risk mitigation — a documented security disclosure process.

how.title: How the audit runs
how.sub: The onboarding wizard turns signup into a delivered report.

steps.title: Create a project
steps.desc: Name your project in the setup wizard — it becomes the home for your audit reports.

steps.title: Connect repos & tickets
steps.desc: Attach GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket or Azure DevOps repos and your Jira / Monday board.

steps.title: Run the audit
steps.desc: A ticket is filed for the security agent, which scans your repos and opens a remediation PR.

steps.title: Read your report
steps.desc: You're notified when it's ready — open the scored SOC 2 report on your project.

family.title: One flow, five system audits
family.sub: The same one-click audit runs across every dimension of your engineering org.

items.name: SOC 2 Readiness
items.desc: Repos and controls mapped to the Common Criteria (CC1–CC9).

items.name: Architecture
items.desc: Design-principle adherence: DRY, SOLID, DDD, and patterns.

items.name: Quality
items.desc: Testing, CI, and build-integrity signals across your repos.

items.name: Product Vision & Roadmap
items.desc: Objectives, key results, roadmap, and a documented vision.

items.name: Privacy & Data-Law
items.desc: GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, and CAN-SPAM readiness from your code.

faq.question: Is this a SOC 2 certification?
faq.answer: No — it's a readiness audit. It produces an automated, evidence-backed report mapped to the SOC 2 Common Criteria so you know exactly what to close before a formal Type I or Type II examination.

faq.question: How does the audit run during signup?
faq.answer: The onboarding wizard creates a project, connects your ticket system and repositories, then files a ticket for the security agent. The audit scores an instant report and dispatches the agent to open a remediation pull request — you're notified when it's ready.

faq.question: Which repositories can it scan?
faq.answer: GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket and Azure DevOps — one or many per project. Tokens stay server-side; the audit reads the repository tree to derive its signals.

faq.question: What other audits are included?
faq.answer: The same flow runs an Architecture analysis, a Quality audit, a Product Vision & Roadmap audit, and a Privacy & Data-Law (GDPR / CCPA / CAN-SPAM) audit — each a scored project report.

finalCta.title: Get your SOC 2 report today
finalCta.sub: Sign up, connect a repo, and your first readiness report lands in minutes.
